text: Sources and rates of production of tritium in a 1000-MW(e) MSBR 2
A 1000-MW(e) Molten-Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) is expected to produce 2420 Ci/day of tritium.
As much as $60\%$ of the tritium produced may be transported to the reactor steam system (assuming no retention by the secondary coolant salt), where it would be released to the environment.
A series of 5 experiments - 3 transient and 2 steady-state experiments - was conducted from July of 1975 through June of 1976 where tritium was added to the CSTF.

Results from the experiments indicated that over $90\%$ of tritium added at steady-state conditions was trapped by sodium fluoroborate and appeared in the off-gas system in a chemically combined (water-soluble) form and that a total of $\sim 98\%$ of the tritium added at steady-state conditions was removed through the off-gas system overall.

Extrapolating to MSBR conditions based on a concentration ratio of $\sim 4000$ for chemically combined tritium to elemental tritium in the salt observed at steady-state conditions, calculations indicated that less than 10 Ci/day of tritium would be transported to the reactor steam system.

A 1000-MW(e) Molten-Salt Breeder Reactor (MSBR) operating at 2250 MW(t) is expected to produce tritium at a rate of 2420 Ci per full-power day. $^{1}$ The major source (Table 1) of tritium is the neutron reactions with lithium, an important constituent of the MSBR fuel salt, which contains uranium tetrafluoride and thorium tetrafluoride dissolved in a lithium fluoride-beryllium fluoride carrier salt.
The composition of the fuel salt, expressed in mole percent of each constituent, is 71.7 LiF, 16 BeF $_2$ , 12 ThF $_4$ , and 0.3 UF $_4$ .
Sources and rates of production of tritium in a 1000-MW(e) MSBR

Production rate (Ci/day)
Ternary fission31
\( ^6\text{Li}(\text{n},\alpha)^3\text{H} \)1210
\( ^7\text{Li}(\text{n},\text{n}\alpha)^3\text{H} \)1170
\( ^{19}\text{F}(\text{n},^{17}\text{O})^2\text{H} \)9
Total2420
At the operating temperatures, 700-977 K (800-1300°F), of an MSBR, tritium tends to diffuse through the metal walls of the various systems.
Early calculations² indicated as much as 1425 Ci/day, or $60\%$ , of the tritium produced would be transported to the reactor steam system assuming no retention of tritium by the secondary salt.
Release of the 1425 Ci/d of tritium would be unacceptably high.
The MSBR design limit objective for release was set at 2 Ci/day.

The resulting concentration of tritium in the water released to the environment would be well below established guidelines<sup>3</sup> for "as low as reasonably achievable" for release of radioactive material in effluents from light water reactors.
This reactor was operated from 1965 to 1969 at a full power of about $7.3\mathrm{MW(t)}$ .
It operated at a maximum temperature of $927\mathrm{K}$ ( $1210^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) with a fuel salt similar to the MSBR fuel salt.

The calculated production rate was 54 Ci/day, and the observed disposition of tritium, not including retention in the off-gas system, amounted to $80\%$ of this production rate: $48\%$ discharging from fuel off-gas system, $2\%$ discharging from coolant off-gas system, $7\%$ discharging in coolant radiator air, $9\%$ appearing in cell atmosphere, and $14\%$ going into the core graphite.

The difference in tritium production between the MSRE at 7.4 Ci day $^{-1}$ MW(t) $^{-1}$ and the MSBR at 1.1 Ci day $^{-1}$ MW(t) $^{-1}$ is due mainly to the increased $^6$ Li concentration that was present in the MSRE fuel salt.

section: 1.3 Proposal for Limitation of Tritium Transport
text: Sodium fluoroborate, the proposed MSBR coolant salt, is an eutectic mixture containing 92 mole $\%$ $\mathrm{NaBF}_4$ and 8 mole $\%$ NaF.

section: 1.3 Proposal for Limitation of Tritium Transport
text: Laboratory experiments<sup>4</sup> indicated that deuterium, on contacting the salt, is retained by the salt because of isotopic exchange with this impurity.

The facility was designed with the capability to supply coolant salt to a number of side loops for the purpose of (1) extending engineering-scale operating experience with the proposed MSBR coolant salt and its cover gas, (2) investigating on-line corrosion product traps, (3) investigating on-line salt monitoring by electrochemical means, and (4) monitoring the corrosion characteristics of the system by means of surveillance specimens.

In February of 1973, the CSTF was placed in standby because of the cancellation of the MSR program.
From February 1973 through March 1974, the system was maintained in standby, namely at room temperature with a static pressure of 5 psig helium blanketing the salt-containing portions.
In April 1974, the MSR program was restarted and work was begun on recommissioning the CSTF in preparation to study tritium behavior in sodium fluoroborate.
The two orifices in series permitted operation of the loop at maximum conditions of temperature [894 K (1150°F)] and pump speed without significant cavitation.

During the initial operating period in 1972, salt was circulated for $\sim 1060$ hr.
Since the CSTF was recommissioned in 1974, salt has been circulated $\sim 8240$ hr.
On July 12, 1976, the loop was shut down following the conclusion of the final tritium experiment and work was begun to decommission the CSTF.

The Hastelloy N tube was about $120\mathrm{mm}$ (4.7 in.) long with an OD of $12.7\mathrm{mm}$ (0.5 in.), and a wall thickness of $1.07\mathrm{mm}$ (0.04 in.), and the provision was made to fasten metallurgical surveillance specimens to the upstream face.
The tritium-hydrogen mixture was fed from the cylinder through the purifier until the $500~\mathrm{cm}^3$ volume was charged with purified gas to the desired pressure.
Purified gas from the $500~\mathrm{cm}^3$ volume was used to charge the addition tube and addition assembly.

For a short-term experiment, the reference volume $(\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{r}})$ and addition tube volume $(\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{p}})$ could be charged directly without using the $500 - \mathrm{cm}^3$ volume.
The normal method was to use a "filter stick," wherein the salt was forced into the sample tube through a porous copper frit having a $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ mean pore size.

For water-soluble (combined) and elemental tritium in the off-gas stream, a $100\text{-cm}^3/\text{min}$ sample stream was taken from the 2-liter/min off-gas stream at a point just downstream of the pump bowl and was passed through a salt mist filter, a needle valve, a water trap to collect water-soluble (combined) tritium, a CuO furnace to convert elemental tritium to tritiated water, and a water trap and cold trap to collect the tritiated water (Fig.
About $20~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{min}$ of hydrogen was added to the sample stream to promote efficient oxidation of the elemental tritium.
The off-gas lines leading to the sampling equipment were maintained at $370\mathrm{K}$ $(200^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ to prevent the water-soluble (combined) tritium from condensing in the lines.
When the system was not in the sampling mode, the $100\mathrm{-cm}^{3}/\mathrm{min}$ sample flow was returned to the main off-gas flow via the sample gas bypass line.
The surface area of loop piping shrouded by the lower cooling duct was approximately $20\%$ of the total surface area of loop piping.
This tap was connected to a diaphragm pump that circulated sample gas at a rate of about $1700~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{min}$ and vented the gas into the loop enclosure.
At the pump discharge, where the pressure was controlled at an absolute pressure of $140\mathrm{kPa}$ , a $100\mathrm{-cm}^3/\mathrm{min}$ side stream was taken off for sampling.

The sample stream was mixed with $20~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{min}$ of humidified helium, and the combined streams were passed through a CuO furnace to convert the tritium to tritiated water and then through a water trap and cold trap to collect the tritiated water.
A probe was inserted in the main salt stream prior to experiment T4 at a point about $1.25\mathrm{m}$ downstream from the salt pump discharge (Fig.
2) to measure the partial pressure of elemental hydrogen and ultimately determine the dissolved elemental tritium concentration in the salt.

The probe (similar in design to the addition tube) consisted of a sealed Hastelloy N tube, $12.7\mathrm{mmOD} \times 1.1\mathrm{mm}$ wall, connected to the necessary valving and instrumentation to permit pressure readings inside the probe in the range of 0.01 to $100\mathrm{Pa}$ (0.1 to $1000\mu \mathrm{mHg}$ ).
The surface area of the addition probe was $50~\mathrm{cm}^2$ and the surface area of the MSBR primary heat exchanger would be $50 \times 10^{6}~\mathrm{cm}^{2}$ .
The addition rate or transfer rate of gas in the experiment was about 10 times less than under MSBR conditions in terms of $\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{hr}$ .
Therefore, the flux of gas in terms of $\mathrm{cm}^3$ of gas per $\mathrm{cm}^2$ of surface area per hr was about $10^5$ times greater in the experiments than under MSBR conditions.

The pressure obtained from the pressure gauges was corrected for (1) the difference between measurements for hydrogen and air since the gauges are calibrated for air and (2) thermal transpiration effects<sup>6</sup> which result since the partial pressure of interest was measured at 811 K (the operating temperature of the loop for the experiments) and the gauges recording the measurement were at room temperature.
The three transient experiments - T1, T2, and T3 - were run during July, August, and September of 1975, respectively.
The two steady-state experiments - T4 and T5 - were conducted for approximately 65 days from February through April of 1976 and for approximately 48 days from May through June of 1976, respectively.
Tritium was added for about 10 hr in each of the three transient experiments.

Thus, about 85 mCi of tritium were added to the loop at a rate of $\sim 7.9$ mCi/hr.
Samples of the salt and loop off-gas were taken during the addition and for 15 days following the addition.

After the results from experiments T2 and T3 were analyzed where trapping efficiencies of $50\%$ and $55\%$ , respectively, were determined, the trapping efficiency of experiment T1 was re-evaluated.
Based on the 12-hr half-life, a trapping efficiency of $60\%$ was determined.
The $60\%$ value for the trapping efficiency appears to be more consistent with the values of experiments T2 and T3.

Although no measure of elemental tritium concentration in the salt is available, a value can be inferred from the concentration of elemental tritium in the off-gas by (1) assuming that the elemental tritium in the off-gas samples represents release from the salt and only from the salt, and (2) assigning reasonable values to gas stripping parameters in the CSTF pump tank.
Calculating the concentration of elemental tritium dissolved in the salt in this way indicated that the ratio of combined/elemental tritium in the salt was about 50.
7) did not increase significantly until after the addition was completed and then rose to $\sim 1750 \mathrm{pCi/cm}^3$ before decreasing in an irregular manner.

Based on the results from experiment T1 it was apparent that the sampling frequency in later experiments would have to be increased, especially for the 24-36 hr following the addition, to reduce the scatter and uncertainty associated with some of the results in order to permit a meaningful evaluation of the experiment.
In experiment T2 approximately $34.3~\mathrm{cm}^3$ of hydrogen at $\sim 1100$ ppm tritium concentration was added to the CSTF for 10.4 hr.
Thus, about 97 mCi of tritium was added to the system at a rate of $\sim 9.3$ mCi/hr.
Samples of the salt and off-gas were taken during the addition period and the following 16 days.

Assuming that the 12-hr half-life completely describes the processes for removal from the salt, a trapping efficiency of $50\%$ was calculated.
Consequently, a single first-order process with the 12-hr half-life was used to describe both the removal of tritium from the salt and the initial buildup.

However, neglecting the longer time constant and using only the 12-hr half-life provide a reasonable basis for calculating the expected rate of tritium buildup in the salt.

Numerical integration of the water-soluble tritium data over $\sim 240$ hr from the start of the addition yielded a total flow of 58 mCi through the off-gas line during the removal period and 7.5 mCi during the addition period.
Thus, a total of 65.5 mCi or about $65\%$ of the tritium added is accounted for as combined tritium in the off-gas stream during this test.

(Numerical integration of the elemental off-gas data showed that an insignificant amount of tritium in this form had appeared in the off-gas system.) Calculations revealed that only $\sim 50$ mCi of tritium had been released from the salt in the combined form.
The metal walls of the loop could possibly retain a portion of the remaining $35\%$ unaccounted for.
In experiment T3 approximately $30.5~\mathrm{cm}^3$ of hydrogen at $\sim 615$ ppm tritium concentration was added to the CSTF for 9.9 hr.
As a result, about 49 mCi of tritium was added to the system at a rate of 4.9 mCi/hr.

The tritium concentration in the salt peaked at $\sim 40$ nCi/g at the end of the addition period and decreased in a manner similar to that observed in the previous two tests.
If two first-order processes for removal of tritium from the salt are assumed, the apparent half-lives for the processes are 8.7 and 108 hr respectively.
The tritium trapping efficiency of the salt determined by the time constants associated with the two half-lives was $55\%$ .
Recalling that the trapping efficiencies of experiments T1 and T2 were $60\%$ and $50\%$ , respectively, the trapping efficiencies then for the three experiments are reasonably consistent.

Using the 8.7- and 108-hr values for the half-lives, the same comparison as was made in experiment T2 was made between the calculated and observed inventories of tritium in the salt as functions of time during the
Elemental tritium in the off-gas reached a maximum concentration of $30~\mathrm{pCi/cm}^3$ , which was significantly higher than in experiment T2 relative to the tritium concentration in the salt.
The elemental tritium concentration in the salt, which is inferred from the elemental tritium concentration in the off-gas, led to a combined/elemental tritium ratio in the salt of 380.
The combined tritium concentration in the off-gas system at the end of the addition was $\sim 10,200\mathrm{pCi/cm}^3$ .
The half-life of the more rapid process ranged from 7-15 hr, while the half-life for the longer process was about 70 hr.

Experiment T4 - the first steady-state experiment - was started on February 3, 1976, and was completed on April 9, 1976.
An attempt was made to start this experiment on January 27, 1976, using an addition tube pressure of $560\mathrm{kPa}$ (80 psia) to obtain an addition rate of $6\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{hr}$ .
This bypass rate was $\sim 14$ times the rate at which tritium was reaching the circulating salt.
Based on the results of those tests, the addition-tube pressure was adjusted to $35\mathrm{kPa}$ (5 psia) in order to
keep the bypass leakage at an acceptable value (1.5 cm³/hr).
The tritium concentration of the addition gas was increased to compensate for the reduced gas addition rate (1.5 cm³/hr).

On March 23, the flow rate of gas was lowered by a factor of 2, to $17~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{s}$ (1 liter/min).
The concentrations of combined tritium in the salt and off-gas increased to about $1350~\mathrm{nCi/g}$ and $340~\mathrm{nCi/cm}^3$ , respectively.

Concentrations of elemental tritium in the cooling-duct air increased to $0.4 - 0.5~\mathrm{pCi/cm}^3$ compared to $0.2 - 0.3~\mathrm{pCi/cm}^3$ concentrations prior to the change of off-gas flow rate.
The elemental tritium concentration in the off-gas still, however, leveled out at about $16~\mathrm{nCi}/\mathrm{cm}^3$ .
Thus, the various concentrations increased approximately a factor of 2 as would have been expected if equilibrium conditions existed.

When the purge-gas flow rate was increased to $68~\mathrm{cm}^3/\mathrm{s}$ (4 liters/min) on March 30, the concentrations of tritium in the off-gas decreased by more than the expected factor of 2 from the original steady-state condition.
system was only about $50\%$ complete.
This suggests that, at the $68\text{-cm}^3/$ spurge-gas flow rate, the gas stripping efficiency in the pump bowl is substantially reduced.

After the addition of tritium to the CSTF was stopped, sampling was continued for only 24 hr into the stripping portion of the experiment so as to have ample time to prepare and conduct another steady-state experiment before having to shut the loop down.

However, the tritium concentration levels were judged to be low enough (~10% of steady-state values obtained in experiment T4) to start experiment T5.

section: 6.1 Buildup and Attainment of Steady-State Conditions
text: Subsequently measurements (those in June, 1976) with the probe showed that the rate of pressure rise in the probe was considerably more rapid than
A total of $120\mathrm{g}$ of sodium metaborate $\left(\mathrm{NaBO}_2\right)$ was added to the CSTF on May 26, 1976.

The purpose of this addition was to increase the free oxide (total amount of oxide less that bound as hydroxide) content of the salt by about $100\mathrm{ppm}$ and to determine any effect on the trapping efficiency of the salt since oxide species in the salt were believed to be involved with the trapping process.
The free oxide content of the salt prior to the $\mathrm{NaBO}_2$ addition was between 410 and $450\mathrm{ppm}$ .
The tritium concentration in the salt increased from a preaddition level of 2000 nCi/g to about 2200-2300 nCi/g.

The tritium concentration subsequently leveled out at about 2500 nCi/g and maintained this level until the addition was stopped, being interrupted only by the transient effects of the $\mathsf{B}_2\mathsf{O}_3$ addition.
The off-gas concentrations returned to their preaddition steady-state levels $\nu 2$ days later.
The tritium concentration in the salt increased from $\sim 2200$ to 2500 nCi/g about June 1, 1976.

The elemental tritium concentration in the off-gas on June 4, 1976, decreased and subsequently increased to a concentration $\sim 1.5$ times higher than the $20\text{-nCi/cm}^3$ concentration observed at steady-state conditions and after the $\mathrm{NaBO}_2$ addition.
On June 10, 1976, $\sim 55$ g of boron oxide ( $B_{2}O_{3}$ ) was added to the CSTF.
The purpose of the $B_{2}O_{3}$ addition was to further increase the oxide level of the salt.
Also, the $B_{2}O_{3}$ was not completely anhydrous and, as a result, a small amount of water was added with the $B_{2}O_{3}$ .
The oxide level of the salt after the $B_{2}O_{3}$ addition was $\sim 710$ ppm compared to $\sim 570$ ppm before the $B_{2}O_{3}$ addition.
The tritium concentration in the salt increased from 2500 to $\sim 4000$ nCi/g immediately after the $\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ addition.
The water-soluble tritium in the off-gas increased (from $\sim 600$ to $\sim 1000$ nCi/cm³) in a similar manner as the salt did, while after the $\mathrm{NaBO}_{2}$ addition it decreased.

The elemental tritium concentration in the off-gas decreased (from $\sim 30$ to $\sim 15$ nCi/cm³) immediately after the $\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ addition, as it had done after the $\mathrm{NaBO}_{2}$ addition.

(Apparently, the $\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ addition interrupted the establishment of a new concentration level of elemental tritium in the off-gas which had begun just before the $\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ addition.) The tritium concentrations in the cooling duct air increased from $\sim 0.7$ to $1.0\mathrm{pCi/cm}^3$ after the $\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ addition.
The magnitude of the transient effect associated with the $\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ addition was greater than that resulting from the $\mathrm{NaBO}_{2}$ addition.
Some 3-4 days were required for the concentrations to return to their pre- $\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ addition levels.

The inventory of tritium in the salt before the $\mathsf{B}_2\mathsf{O}_3$ addition associated with the 2000 nCi/g concentration was $\sim 1$ Ci of tritium, and correspondingly for the 4000 nCi/g concentration resulting after the addition of $\mathsf{B}_2\mathsf{O}_3$ the inventory of tritium in the salt was 2 Ci.
The addition rate for experiment T5 was only $\sim 83$ mCi/hr.
(This undefined reservoir is discussed further in Section 7.)

The helium leak detector, as mentioned earlier, was connected to the hydrogen partial pressure probe for flow rate measurements of mass 4 species - presumably the mass of an HT molecule after hydrogen and tritium atoms permeated the walls of the probe.
This was not the case, however, as significant partial pressures of total hydrogen were observed after the two chemical additions (Table 8, p.
On June 7, 1976, a flow rate of $5.0 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~cm}^3/\mathrm{s}$ of mass 4 species was recorded with the helium leak detector.
Based on the hydrogen partial pressure probe measurement for the three days preceding this helium leak detector reading, a flow rate of $6.0 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~cm}^3/\mathrm{s}$ was calculated.

Similarly, on June 18, 1976, a helium leak detector reading of $2.2 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~cm}^3/\mathrm{s}$ compared favorably with a flow rate of $4.0 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~cm}^3$ determined from the probe measurement made during June 14-16, 1976.

Even though the partial pressure of total hydrogen may have been artificially high at the time the measurements with the two devices were made (due to a leak in the connection between the helium leak detector and hydrogen partial probe discussed earlier in Section 6.3), the agreement obtained with the measurements by the two devices serves to substantiate the 0.4-Pa $(3\mu \mathrm{mHg})$ total hydrogen partial pressure obtained at steady-state conditions during experiments T4 and T5.

section: 7. INTERCOMPARISON AND INTERPRETATION OF EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS
text: The ratio of combined tritium in the salt to elemental in the salt of $\sim 4300$ determined for experiments T4 and T5 was the result of a more direct measurement than those ratios obtained for the transient experiments (Table 3), which were significantly lower.

Based on a value of $\sim 4000$ for the combined to elemental ratio of tritium in sodium fluoroborate observed in CSTF tritium addition experiments T4 and T5, calculations were performed to examine the resulting behavior and distribution of tritium in an MSBR assuming this same ratio were established in the secondary salt of an MSBR.
The same computer program was used for this calculation as was used for earlier calculations that indicated as much as 1425 Ci/day of tritium could be transported to the steam system of an MSBR.
Modified case where a value of $\sim 4000$ for the concentration ratio of combined to elemental tritium in the salt was established in the reactor secondary or coolant system.

Furthermore, for the modified case, an $80\%$ efficiency was assumed for removal of the chemically combined form (water-soluble form in off-gas of CSTF) on a $10\%$ salt bypass stream $(0.5\mathrm{m}^3/\mathrm{s} - \mathrm{MSBR}$ reference value).

Modified case where a value of $\sim 4000$ for the concentration ratio of combined to elemental tritium in the salt as determined from CSTF tritium experiments was established in the reactor secondary or coolant system.

For case 2, an $80\%$ efficiency for removal of chemically combined form from a $10\%$ salt bypass steam $(0.5\mathrm{m}^3/\mathrm{s})$ is assumed.

In case 2, representing the extrapolation from results obtained from the CSTF tritium experiments to MSBR conditions, only $0.16\%$ (4 Ci/day) of the 2420 Ci/day of tritium produced in an MSBR was transported to the reactor steam system compared with 1425 Ci/day of tritium reaching the reactor steam system in the reference case.
The release rate of tritium for the MSBR includes the entire fuel cycle, while that for PWRs would be about 80 Ci/day if releases associated with fuel reprocessing were included.

However, shortly after this series of experiments was begun, funding for the MSR program was withdrawn, effective at the end of fiscal year 1976.
More than $90\%$ of tritium added to the CSTF at steady-state conditions appeared in the off-gas system in a chemically combined (water-soluble) form.

Therefore, sodium fluoroborate is considered the leading candidate<sup>11</sup> as a secondary coolant for an MSBR and would be expected to adequately limit the transport of tritium to the reactor steam system and subsequent release to the environment.
